Chapter 1: Why R for Management Consultants

R for Management Consultants | BK Advisors

BK Advisors

2026-02-28

Learning Objectives

After this chapter, you will be able to:

  • Explain why R is a valuable tool for management consultants in global health
  • Navigate the RStudio interface (console, script editor, environment, files)
  • Execute basic R commands in the console
  • Understand the difference between R scripts and the console
  • Install and load R packages

Chapter Overview

Excel
Limits
→
Why
R?
→
RStudio
Tour
→
First
Commands
→
Packages

The Limits of Spreadsheets

Why Excel is not enough for serious analysis

The Spreadsheet Trap

A consultant working on an MNH program across five countries receives monthly DHIS2 data extracts.

  • Each country sends a different Excel format, each month a new file
  • With 60 Excel files a year, the consultant spends more time copying and pasting than analyzing
  • One misplaced column, one accidental overwrite, and a month of work is compromised

If your analysis can’t be repeated exactly by someone else clicking one button, it’s not reproducible — and in global health, non-reproducible analysis is a liability.

Excel vs. R: A Practical Comparison

Task Excel R
Combine 60 monthly data files Copy-paste, error-prone 3 lines of code
Recalculate when data updates Redo everything manually Re-run the script
Audit trail None (who changed cell B47?) Full version history
Handle 100,000+ rows Slows down, crashes No problem
Reproduce for a colleague “Let me walk you through…” “Run this script”

When to Keep Using Excel

  • Quick look at a small dataset (fewer than 100 rows)
  • One-off calculations that won’t be repeated
  • Sharing with stakeholders who need to edit values interactively

R doesn’t replace Excel. It replaces the parts of Excel that waste your time and introduce errors.

What Makes R Different

Free, reproducible, and built for data

R in One Sentence

R is a free, open-source programming language designed for statistical computing and data visualization. It’s used by epidemiologists, economists, data scientists, and — increasingly — management consultants.

  • Free and open-source (no license fees, ever)
  • 20,000+ packages for every analytical task imaginable
  • Publication-quality charts with ggplot2
  • Reproducible reports with Quarto (this course!)
  • Active community of global health researchers and analysts

Who Uses R in Global Health?

WHO

WHO uses R for disease surveillance dashboards and epidemic modeling.

IHME

The Global Burden of Disease study (IHME) runs on R.

DHS Program

DHS Program produces country-level MNH indicator reports using R.

Donor Requirements

Many donors now require reproducible analysis pipelines — R delivers this.

The RStudio Tour

Your new workspace

The Four Panes of RStudio

Pane Location Purpose
Source Editor Top-left Write and save R scripts
Console Bottom-left Run commands interactively
Environment Top-right See your data and variables
Files / Plots / Help Bottom-right Browse files, view charts, read documentation

Think of the Source Editor as your notebook and the Console as your calculator. Write in the notebook, test in the calculator.

Your First R Commands

# R as a calculator
2 + 3
10 * 5.5
100 / 7

# Storing a value
maternal_deaths <- 45
total_deliveries <- 1200

# Computing a rate
mmr <- (maternal_deaths / total_deliveries) * 100000
mmr

Naming Things Well

# Good names — descriptive, lowercase, underscores
neonatal_deaths <- 23
facility_count <- 48
anc_coverage_pct <- 0.72

# Bad names — unclear, inconsistent
x <- 23          # What is x?
FC <- 48          # Abbreviations confuse
ANCcov <- 0.72   # Mixed case is hard to read

You’ll read your code more often than you write it. Name variables so your future self — or your colleague — can understand them without comments.

Scripts vs. Console

Write once, run forever

Console: The Calculator

  • The console runs one command at a time
  • Results disappear when you close RStudio
  • Great for quick exploration and testing
  • Like scratch paper — useful, but not permanent

Scripts: The Notebook

An R script (.R file) saves every command you write.

  • Run the entire script to reproduce your analysis
  • Share with colleagues — they get identical results
  • The foundation of reproducible analysis
# ── MNH Indicator Calculation ──────────────────────
# This script calculates key MNH indicators
# from facility-level delivery data.

# Input data
live_births <- 1150
stillbirths <- 50
neonatal_deaths <- 28
maternal_deaths <- 3
total_deliveries <- live_births + stillbirths

# Calculate indicators
stillbirth_rate <- (stillbirths / total_deliveries) * 1000
nmr <- (neonatal_deaths / live_births) * 1000
mmr <- (maternal_deaths / live_births) * 100000

# Display results
stillbirth_rate  # per 1,000 total deliveries
nmr              # per 1,000 live births
mmr              # per 100,000 live births

Packages — Extending R

Standing on the shoulders of 20,000 packages

What Are Packages?

A package is a collection of functions that someone else has written and shared. Installing a package is like adding a new app to your phone — it gives R new capabilities.

  • install.packages("dplyr") — download once (like buying an app)
  • library(dplyr) — load each session (like opening the app)

The Packages We’ll Use

Package Purpose Chapter
readr Import CSV files 3
dplyr Transform data (filter, summarize) 4
tidyr Reshape data (pivot) 5
ggplot2 Create charts 6–7
scales Format axes (%, commas) 7
patchwork Combine multiple charts 7
knitr Tables in reports 9 
# Install the tidyverse (includes dplyr, ggplot2, tidyr, readr, stringr)
install.packages("tidyverse")

# Load it
library(tidyverse)

The tidyverse is a collection of packages designed to work together. Installing it gives you most of what you need for this entire course.

Key Takeaways

R replaces the repetitive, error-prone parts of Excel with reproducible scripts.

RStudio organizes your workflow into four panes: editor, console, environment, and files/plots.

Packages extend R’s capabilities. The tidyverse is your core toolkit for this course.

Looking Ahead

Next: Chapter 2 — R Building Blocks

  • Vectors: storing multiple values
  • Data types: numeric, character, logical, factor
  • Functions: reusable calculations
  • Getting help: ?function_name and online resources
